perturbation.original_text: 11. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Without your prior written consent, we will not for a period of one year from the date hereof directly or indirectly solicit for employment any person who is now employed by you or any of your subsidiaries who is identified by us as a result of our invastigation of the Business. 11. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to a section discussing non-solicitation obscures its importance and makes it less easily discoverable. Governing law clauses should be conspicuously placed to be effective.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law requires clear and conspicuous choice of law provisions for enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law and common law require that choice of law provisions are clear and conspicuous to be enforceable. Burying this clause diminishes its clarity and discoverability, potentially rendering it unenforceable. 1 Pa. Code § 31.5 outlines general filing requirements, which underscores the importance of conspicuous presentation of legal documents. While not directly on choice of law, it highlights general Pennsylvania regulatory expectations for clarity. Case law further requires the language to be unambiguous.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 1 Pa. Code § 31.5. Communications and filings generally. § 31.5. Communications and filings generally. (a) Communications, submittals and pleadings should be addressed to the agency at the office of the agency unless otherwise specifically directed. Communication and filings should clearly designate the file number, docket number or similar identifying symbols, if any, employed by the agency, and should set forth a short title. The person communicating shall state his address, the party he represents and how response should be sent to him if not by first class mail. (b) Submittals and pleadings, including documents filed under Chapter 35 (relating to formal proceedings) shall comply with Chapter 33 (relating to documentary filings) and the applicable provisions of this chapter. (c) In a proceeding when upon inspection the agency is of the opinion that a submittal or pleading tendered for filing does not comply with this part and any applicable regulations of the agency or, if it is an application or similar document, does not sufficiently set forth required material or is otherwise insufficient, the agency may decline to accept the document for filing and may return it unfiled, or the agency may accept it for filing and advise the person tendering it of the deficiency and require that the deficiency be corrected. (d) The agency may order any redundant, immaterial, impertinent or scandalous matter stricken from a document filed with it. Cross References This section cited in 1 Pa. Code § 33.61 (relating to applications for waiver of formal requirements); 4 Pa. Code § 615.5 (relating to filings with Docket Clerk); 7 Pa. Code § 179.3 (relating to filings and appeals); 16 Pa. Code § 42.12 (relating to caption); 19 Pa. Code § 13.3 (relating to communications and filings generally); 34 Pa. Code § 111.11 (relating to content and form); 34 Pa. Code § 111.32 (relating to form/content); 34 Pa. Code § 131.11 (relating to filing, service and proof of service); 34 Pa Code § 131.21 (relating to i
perturbation.justification: The change moves the governing law clause, potentially obscuring its importance. The Perturbation Explanation states that governing law clauses should be conspicuously placed. The Law Explanation states that Pennsylvania law requires choice of law provisions to be clear and conspicuous to be enforceable. Snippet 1, 1 Pa. Code § 31.5, outlines general filing requirements, which underscores the importance of conspicuous presentation of legal documents in Pennsylvania. By moving the clause, its clarity and discoverability are diminished. This constitutes a structural flaw that undermines its enforceability, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7. We acknowledge that you make no express or implied representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of the Information, and you expressly disclaim any and all liability that may be based on the Information, errors therein or omissions therefrom. We agree that we are not entitled to rely on the accuracy or completeness of the Information and that we shall be entitled to rely solely on the representations and warranties made to us by the Business in any final purchase agreement regarding the acquisition.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. The Information will be kept confidential and shall not without your prior written consent, be disclosed by us, or by our agents, representatives or employees, in any manner whatsover, in whole or in part, and shall not be used by us, our agents, representatives or employees, other than in connection with the transaction described above. 7. We acknowledge that you make no express or implied representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of the Information, and you expressly disclaim any and all liability that may be based on the Information, errors therein or omissions therefrom. We agree that we are not entitled to rely on the accuracy or completeness of the Information and that we shall be entitled to rely solely on the representations and warranties made to us by the Business in any final purchase agreement regarding the acquisition.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclaimer of warranties and liability to be in the first section buries the disclaimer within a confidentiality clause. Disclaimers should be prominently displayed.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) requires disclaimers of warranty to be conspicuous.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania's adoption of the UCC requires warranty disclaimers to be conspicuous to be effective. Hiding this clause in the general introductory section compromises its conspicuousness, making it potentially unenforceable. Specifically, 13 Pa.C.S. § 2316 requires that disclaimers are written so that a reasonable person against whom it is to operate ought to have noticed it.
perturbation.justification: The Perturbation Explanation states that moving the disclaimer to the first section buries it within a confidentiality clause, meaning it is not prominently displayed. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation indicate that the disclaimer's conspicuousness is compromised, potentially making it unenforceable under UCC, which Pennsylvania has adopted. This constitutes a structural flaw where a required legal disclosure is placed in a misleading section. Without any law snippets to use as evidence, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation sufficiently describe a Structural Flaw Legal Contradiction.
